title: The Invisible Teacher And His Disciples
author: Unknown

Blumenkranz shanirb@hotmail.com 5 Abstracts and Bios
(in the order of presentation)

Eliot Wolfson—Mundus Imaginalis and the Testof the Veil: Linear Circularity of Time in
Corbin’s Phenomenology of Imagination
Henry Corbin’sexplanation of the testof the veil in the Ṣūfi tradition, the one with mystical intuition
unearths that the world is naught but the concealmentof the divine concealment, and hence through
the veil one sees the veil and thereby discerns that God is present in the very world from which God
is absent. The cosmos, accordingly, is the mirror whereby the divine light sees itself through that
which is allegedly other than itself. From the spiritual topography of the Andalusian mystic and poet
Ibn ‘Arabī, Corbin deduces that all creation is an epiphany, the manifestation of the unmanifest, the
passage from occultation to revelation, and as such it is an actof the divine imagination. However, the
disclosure of the hidden perforce requires that the hidden remains hidden in its disclosure otherwise
it is not adisclosure of the hidden. The imagination, therefore, as Corbin noted, can reveal the hidden
only by continuing to veil it. Once transparency is achieved, the visionary can discriminate between
the hidden and the manifest, but at adeeper level the imaginal vision transmits the truth of the
coincidentia oppositorum such thatopposites are identical in virtue of their opposition, and hence
there is no manifestation of the imaginative presence that is not at the same time an occlusion. In each
moment, the display of the inapparent being in the manifold beings of the universe, the homologation
of the infinite in finite form, assumes adistinct appearance—indeed, each theophany is anew creation
that is discontinuous with the past and impervious to the causal sequence of outward facts—but the
appearance of the inapparent can appear only to the extent that it persists as inapparent. Eternal time is
thus the measure of the ever-changing appearance of the nonappearance of divine ipseity.
Elliot R. Wolfson, a Fellow of the American Academy of Jewish Research and the American Academy
of Arts and Sciences, is the Marsha and Jay Glazer Endowed Chair in Jewish Studies and Distinguished
Professor of Religion at University of California, Santa Barbara. He is the author of many publications
including most recently The Duplicity of Philosophy’s Shadow: Heidegger, Nazism and the Jewish
Other; Heidegger and Kabbalah: Hidden Gnosis and the Path of Poiēsis; Suffering Time:
Philosophical, Kabbalistic, and Ḥasidic Reflections on Temporality; and Tragedy of Being and
Ontological Untruth: The Philosophical Pathos of Susan Taubes’s Mytheology.

Joan Copjec—Revolution and Profanation
Steven Wasserstrom characterizes the work of Corbin as an attempt to “re-enchant” an overly
rationalized world by injecting amytho-centric vision into it. This paper will argue that it is the act
of profanation performed by the Muslim mystics which Corbin allows us to see. The argument will
unfold through areading of Kiarostami’searly film, Case No. 1, Case No. 2, made just after the Iranian
Revolution and at nearly the same time as Khomeini endorsed cinema in his “Address at Bishisht-iZahara.”
Joan Copjec is Professor of Modern Culture and Media at Brown University. Her primary areas of
research are cinema; psychoanalysis; and continental and Islamic philosophy. Her books include Read
My Desire: Lacan against the Historicists, Imagine There’s No Woman, and Cloud: Between
Paris and Tehran (forthcoming). 6 Holly Friesen, a Canadian artist, was born in Saskatchewan, studied Visual Arts at John Abbott
College in Montreal and painting at York University in Toronto. After many years of travel and
study she settled in Mont-Tremblant, QC where she raised her daughters while continuing to paint
from the forests, rivers, and rocks of the Laurentian landscape. During these years Holly also ran
an art school and created opportunities for local artists to work and exhibit in avariety of diverse
locations. Her work is collected internationally and partof both corporate and private collections.
Holly’spassion is painting the earth from the inside out while collaborating with other artists to
make art more visible in our everyday world.

Daniela Boccassini—The Invisible Teacher and His Disciples: Jung’sand Corbin’s
Approaches to “the Green One”.
Corbin met Jung at Ascona in 1949. In 1953 he published along, insightful review of Jung’s “Answer to
Job.” During the following years, he became so thoroughly committed to the Eranos “paradox” that no one
but he could have pronounced the memorial for Olga Fröbe and Carl Jung in 1962. Yet, for all the respect,
if not devotion, that Corbin expressed to Jung, and for all the sympathy that Jung showed Corbin, their
relationship was never an easy one. As aconsequence, contradictory readings of it have been proposed.
Is there any textual ground that might allow us to see deeper, and more insightfully, into their differences
and commonalities? In this talk, Iexamine Jung’sand Corbin’sapproaches to the figure of al-Khidr/Khezr.
Acomparative reading of their views on this figure of immortal and of trickster may, Ibelieve, help us find
their meeting place, at the confluence of the two seas.
Daniela Boccassini is Professor of Italian Studies at The University of British Columbia (Vancouver,
Canada) and Co-director of the C.G. Jung Society of Vancouver. Her research interests focus on the
quest for transformative understanding or gnosis, as different from argumentative logic. In this
perspective, she has approached the works of Medieval and Renaissance authors such as Dante Alighieri
and Montaigne, ancient transformative practices such as falconry and alchemy, Jung’sand Corbin’s
hermeneutics and worldviews. Among her recent publications: Transmutatio: la via ermetica alla felicità, Oikosophia: from the Intelligence of the Heart to Ecophilosophy, and various articles on
Dante and Jung.

Jeff Kripal—Why the Aliens Don’t Land: The Imaginal in Modern Thought and the
Contemporary Abduction Literature
This essay is meant as an intervention. The category of the “imaginal” is generally credited to the
French Islamicist Henry Corbin. But Corbin did not first coin term. Not even close. He was likely deeply
influenced by the imagination créatrice of C.G. Jung, who in turn derived the idea and practice from his
Swiss mentor Théodore Flournoy, who used it extensively in his masterpiece on mediumship, channeled
romance, and aclaimed reincarnation case, From Indiato the Planet Mars. For the same work,
Flournoy in turn expressly borrowed another related word, the “supernormal,” from the Cambridge
classicist and psychical researcher Frederic Myers, whofirst coined and employed the “imaginal” in
the 1880s in relationship to the supernormal, long before Corbin came on the scene with his Iranian
materials. The original psychical and evolutionary sensibilities of Myers’simaginal are largely absent in
Corbin’swork. The present essay seeks to restore these original meanings through aquick trip through
the twentieth- and now twenty-first century renaissance of the expression’soriginal insectoid meanings
and evolutionary impulses. Toward the same end, Iwill introduce asingle American author in whom
these patterns are especially obvious in both acomedic and amystical theological sense, Jeremy Vaeni. 7

Jeffrey J. Kripal is the Associate Dean of the Faculty and Graduate Programs in the School of the
Humanities and the J. Newton Rayzor Chair in Philosophy and Religious Thought at Rice University.
He is also the Associate Director of the Center for Theory and Research at the Esalen Institute in Big
Sur, California. Jeff is the author of nine books, including, most recently, The Flip: Who You Really Are
and Why It Matters, where he envisions the future centrality and urgency of the humanities in
conversation with the history of science, the philosophy of mind, and our shared ethical, political, and
ecological challenges. His full body of work can be seen at <jeffreyjkripal.com>. He thinks he may be
Spider-Man. Earth Recitals: A Reading by Melissa Kwasny
Melissa Kwasny is the author of seven books of poetry, including Where Outside the Body is the Soul
Today; Pictograph; The Nine Senses; Reading Novalis in Montana; and the
forthcoming The Cloud Path, as well as acollection of essays, Earth Recitals: Essays on Image and Vision. She is also the editor of two anthologies: I Go to the Ruined Place: Contemporary Poets in Defense
of Global Human Rights and Toward the Open Field: Poets on the Artof Poetry 1800-1950.
She lives in western Montana.

Pierre Lory—Henry Corbin, Reader of Ibn ‘Arabi: Some Perspectives
Henry Corbin published in 1958 his book Creative Imagination in the Sufism of Ibn ‘Arabî. The book
was translated into English, Spanish, Italian and Arabic. It has had adefinite
influence on awide range of readerships. Academic studies on Muslim mysticism owe him alot. That
said, the field has evolved considerably since then. Some of Corbin’stheses were the objectof criticism.
In particular, he was accused of separating too clearly the esoteric thoughtof Ibn ‘Arabî from the
common Sunnism, that referring to the Koran, the hadith and the sharia. It was felt that he gave an
exaggerated place to the imagination in the spiritual evolution of the mystic; and that he tended to pull
Ibn ‘Arabî too much in the direction of Shi’ism. It is not at all aquestion here of entering into polemics
or of aggravating debates in asterile way. In themselves, these debates are fruitful, they make thought
progress, and it is in this sense that it is important to return to them. Corbin’stexts have posed in new
terms the relationship between mysticism and philosophy, between Sufism and Shi’ism, and finally,
paradoxically, between mysticism and politics.
Pierre Lory is Professor Emeritus at the Ecole Pratique des Hautes Etudes (Paris), where he taught
Islamic mysticism and esotericism. Among his books: La science des lettres en islam; Le rêve et
ses interprétations en Islam; Alchimie et mystique en terre d’Islam; La dignité de l’homme
face aux anges, aux animaux et aux djinns.

Tom Cheetham—A Twofold Idolatry
For over 30 years I’ve had arecurring obsession with twofootnotes in Corbin’sgreat book on Ibn ‘Arabi.
The firstoccurs in an accountof the “cognitive function of sympathy” in revealing the meaning of
virtual existence. This long note is devoted to explaining the importance of Etienne Souriau’swork for
Corbin. The second appears as partof adiscussion of the doctrine of recurrent creation, and contains
my favorite passage in all of Corbin’swriting. Taken together, they can be read as an explanation of
why Creative Imagination resolves what is perhaps the most importantof the dualisms that give rise to
secular cultures: the gulf between immanence and transcendence. 8 Tom Cheetham, Ph.D., is the author of five books on Henry Corbin and the implications of his
thought: The World Turned Inside Out, Green Man / Earth Angel, After Prophecy,
All the World an Icon, and Imaginal Love.

Chiara Bottici and Philipp Valentini—Rethinking Myths and the Struggle for the Soul with
and after Corbin
The purpose of this paper is to think about myth and the way myths are re-appropriated by those who
wish to embody the truth they find in it. Henry Corbin’soeuvre shall be our main interlocutor in this
walk through the trails of athought that contemplates the power of myth making. Firstly, we would like
to criticize the romantic background of Corbin’snotion of myth as well as the melancholic passion that
animates it. We then reflecton the notion of myth by distinguishing it from philosophy and from what
is perceived by believers as adivine revelation (according to the school that walks in the footsteps of Ibn
‘Arabi’sspeculative Sufism whose teachings Corbin has presented in his book L’Imagination créatrice).
In doing so, we will explore also the relationship between myth and the imaginal world, as distinguished
from both the social imaginary and the individual imagination.
Chiara Bottici is aphilosopher and awriter, known for her philosophy of political imagination and for
her feminist writings. She is Associate Professor of Philosophy at The New School For Social Research,
and is the author, among others, of Imaginal Politics: Images beyond Imagination and The Imaginary, A Philosophy of Political Myth, Anarchafemminism, and A Feminist Mythology.
Philipp Valentini earned his Ph.D. at the University of Fribourg (Switzerland) in 2020. He is currently
a European Postdoctoral Researcher at the New School for Social Research in New York. He is the
editor of the book Esoteric Lacan. His work is at the crossroads of the Western philosophical
canon (Heidegger, Lacan) and Sufi thought and practices (Sadr Din Qunawi, Ibrahim Niasse), which he
approaches from adecolonial and aesthetic perspective.

Trey Spruance—Henry Corbin and the Musical Imaginal of Secret Chiefs 3
For 25 years my music group Secret Chiefs 3 has been developing arigorous compositional and
performative environment that has been profoundly influenced by the writings of Henry Corbin. The
most particular examples of his influence can be found in Secret Chiefs 3’s “Ishraqiyun songbook”
cycles, where intimate musical details are sourced from Corbin’s Eranos lectures: “The Science of
the Balance” (on the writings of Haydar Amuli), “The Configuration of the Temple of the Ka’bah”
(according to Qadi Sa’id Qummi). The first partof the presentation will use aural and visual aids to
illustrate how musical ideas were generated from text and moved to concept, from concept to practice,
and from practice to composition. The second partof the presentation will move from composition to
performance, with asmall trio concertof relevant works.
Trey Spruance, afounding member of the avant garde rock band Mr. Bungle, composes and produces
music for adiverse range of ensembles (Kronos Quartet, LINES Ballet, the Traditional Russian
Orchestra of Krasnoyarsk). He has arranged and produced music for NY composer John Zorn, and
worked as aguitarist and songwriter with rock band Faith No More. An avid autodidact and admirer
of Henry Corbin since the mid 1990s, Spruance’sprincipal pursuitover the decades has been the
developmentof avast repertoire for his chameleon-like ensemble Secret Chiefs 3. Here an enormous
multiverse of harmonic and rhythmic color has been focused into apalette of seven “satellite bands,”
witheach intended as an application of philosophy expressed musically in one of “seven modes” 9

operating in strict pedagogical distinction from the others. Spruance’scontemplative focus on the art
of the recorded listening experience serves these goals by nurturing contrasts between genre, harmonic
structure, musical tradition, production technique etc. That effort works in tandem with the practical
creation of live touring ensembles made up of like-minded musicians, who must also be skilled in
negotiating the poles of musical speculation and practice. “Splinters of a Careless Alphabet”: Reading and Discussion with Roxanne Varzi
Roxanne Varzi is awriter, artist, filmmaker, playwright and Professor of Anthropology at the University
of California Irvine. She holds a Ph.D. in Anthropology from Columbia University, and was the first
Fulbright scholar to do research in Iran since the Revolution. She is the author of Warring Souls: Media,
Martyrdom and Youth in post-Revolution Iran and the 2016 Independent Publishers Gold Medal
winning novel Last Scene Underground: An Ethnographic Novel of Iran. Her film, Plastic Flowers Never
Die is distributed by Documentary Educational Resources. Her short stories have appeared in the
New York Press, Anthropology and Humanism, and in three anthologies of Iranian-American writing.

Todd Lawson—In the Beginning was Metaphor, and Metaphor was with God: Corbin and
the Quran
If we survey the Table of Contents of En Islam iranien it is clear that the central concern of this fourvolume magnum opus is The Book or The Word. Corbin’sdeep involvement with revelation language
is well-known. This presentation will focus on seems to be at the heartof this concern, metaphor. A
survey of the various manifestations of metaphorical expression and method will contextualize Corbin’s
insistence that the Angel of Revelation is also the Angel of Interpretation. Two types of metaphor
frequently encountered in Corbin will be of particular interest: l’amphibolie and l’isomorphisme. These
two metaphoric “literary devices” (and others), may be considered, in turn, an illuminating paradigm
for the method and contents of much of Corbin’swork. In the uncompromisingly apophatic milieu
of Corbin’scentral preoccupations, it is metaphor that provides the imaginative energy and vision,
otherwise referred to as gnosis, revelation, inspiration, and apocalypse. The structure of metaphor, it
may be added, is seen as an analogue for the mystic or gnostic path, one that begins with the known
and moves towards agoal which is necessarily unknown.
Todd Lawson specializes in Islamic Thought, especially Shi’i Quran commentary and philosophical
theology. His most recent book in this area is Intimacy and Ecstasy in Quran Commentary. His
most recent book is Being human: Baha’iperspectives on Islam, Modernity and Peace. Other
publications may be downloaded at <toddlawson.ca>. He is Professor emeritus at the University of
Toronto and lives in Montreal.

Wouter Hanegraaff—Henry Corbin as Knightof the Temple
The topic of this lecture is Corbin’sinvolvement in esoteric neo-Templar Orders and Freemasonry
during the final decade of his life, and its relevance to his major lecture “L’Imago templi face aux normes
profanes” presented at Eranos in 1974. It is not very widely known that Corbin became a Freemason
during the 1970s, practicing aspecific Christian-chivalric system originally created by Jean-Baptiste
Willermoz and known as the Rite Écossais Rectifié. This background is essential for understanding
the Université Saint-Jean de Jérusalem and its relation to esoteric and occultist milieus and traditions
concerned with an “inner church” of the spirit, auniversal Temple accessible only in the mundus
imaginalis, and an esoteric knighthood active behind the scenes of “profane history.” 10 Wouter J. Hanegraaff is Professor of History of Hermetic Philosophy and Related Currents at the
University of Amsterdam, The Netherlands. Among many other publications, he is the author of New
Age Religion and Western Culture: Esotericism in the Mirror of Secular Thought; Esotericism and
the Academy: Rejected Knowledge in Western Culture; and Hermetic Spirituality and the Historical
Imagination: Altered States of Knowledge in Late Antiquity.

Jean-Louis Vieillard-Baron—La place de l’âme du monde dans la pensée d’Henry Corbin
Peu de temps avant sa mort, Henry Corbin avoulu relancer « le combat pour l’âme du monde ». Il l’a
fait en convoquant une session de l’Université Saint-Jean de Jérusalem qu’il avait créée avec quelques
amis, Gilbert Durand (spécialiste de l’anthropologie de l’imaginaire), Richard Stauffer (spécialiste de
la Réforme). L’âme du monde est la présence de Dieu dans le monde; Elle est médiatrice entre Dieu et
l’homme, et ne signifie pas de retour du panthéisme qui confond Dieu et lanature. Le néoplatonisme de
Plotin et de l’empereur Julien apparaît comme une source féconde de la philosophie mystique de la Perse
qu’a révélée Henry Corbin par ses études.
Jean-Louis Vieillard-Baron was born in Tunis in 1944. He earned his License in Philosophy from the
Sorbonne in 1967-1968, and defended his doctoral thesis on “Plato and German Idealism ”
in 1976 at the University of Nanterre. After his national service in Morocco, he met Henry Corbin, and
became amember of the Université Saint-Jean de Jérusalem. Following the death of Henry Corbin, he
helped Stella Corbin in continuing and promoting the work of her husband. He taught the philosophy
and history of religion at the University of Tours from 1973 to 1988, and at the University of Poitiers from
1989 to 2012. He is the author of some 30 books and approximately 300 articles. His recent publications
include Le Problème du temps, études, Le Spiritualisme de Bergson, and Le Spiritualisme
français de Maine de Biran à Emile Boutroux. Secret Chiefs 3: Ishraqiyun trio
Eyvind Kang - Viola d’Amore
Shanir Blumenkranz - Gimbre, Oud
Trey Spruance - Saz, modified guitars 11

Main Conference Location
The conference will be held in the Center for the Study of World Relligions, located
at 42 Francis Ave. Two events will take place in Divinity Hall, and one event at the
American Academy of Arts and Sciences (see following pages for directions). 12 Walking Directions from the CSWR
to Divinity Hall
Two events—“Earth Recitals: A Reading by Melissa Kwasny” on May 12, from 4:30–5:15 pm, and
“Splinters of a Careless Alphabet: Reading and Discussion with Roxanne Varzi” on May 13, from
4:30–5:30 pm—will be held in Emerson Chapel in Divinity Hall, located at 14 Divinity Avenue.
The walk from the CSWR (point A) to Divinity Hall (point B) should take 2-3 minutes. The
entrance to Divinity Hall is from the back of the building, accessible from the courtyard acing
the Bri Biological Labs. Emerson Chapel is located on the 3rd floor of Divinity Hall, and can be
accessed by elevator. 13 Walking Directions from the CSWR to the
American Academy of Arts & Sciences
The Ishraqiyyun trio concerton May 14, from 3:30–4:30 pm, will be held at
the American Academy of Arts & Sciences located at 136 Irving St.
The American Academy is accessible viathe park behind the CSWR. 14 Walking Directions from the Hotel to the CSWR Porter Square Hotel
